key_points: Matter consists of particles which have mass, occupy space, and are in constant motion
Kinetic theory explains properties of solids, liquids, and gases based on particle motion and forces
Intermolecular forces determine the rigidity, fluidity, and compressibility of matter
Diffusion rate depends on temperature, particle size, and medium
Changes of state involve energy transfer without change in chemical composition
Sublimation is the direct change from solid to gas without passing through liquid state
Expansion and contraction of matter occur due to changes in kinetic energy of particles
Behavior of gases can be explained using gas laws (Boyle’s, Charles’ and Avogadro’s laws)
examples: Ammonium chloride subliming directly from solid to gas
Candle wax melting and vaporizing when heated
Diffusion of hydrogen gas in air demonstrates fastest diffusion due to small particle mass
Expansion of mercury in a thermometer with temperature rise shows thermal expansion
Condensation of water vapor on a cold surface demonstrates energy release during state change
formulas: Change in volume due to thermal expansion: ΔV = 3αVΔT (for solids, α = coefficient of linear expansion)
Boyle’s Law: P1V1 = P2V2 (at constant temperature for gases)
Charles’ Law: V1/T1 = V2/T2 (at constant pressure for gases)
exam_tips: Explain particle theory of matter with examples and diagrams
Discuss energy transfer during change of state and diffusion
Understand and apply gas laws for solving numerical problems
Remember key differences between solids, liquids, and gases including compressibility and density
Illustrate sublimation, condensation, evaporation, melting, freezing, and boiling with real-life examples
